items.title: Importance of Detailed History and Physical Examination in Acute Abdominal Pain
items.content: A meticulous history and physical examination are crucial for distinguishing urgent conditions from those that can be managed nonoperatively, focusing the differential diagnosis, and expediting diagnostic evaluation.
keyPoints: Detailed history and physical examination are critically important.
Focuses differential diagnosis.
Allows expedited diagnostic evaluation.
items.reference: Abdominal Pain · Danny O. Jacobs

items.title: Correlation Between Pain Severity and Underlying Condition in Acute Abdominal Pain
items.content: Pain severity does not necessarily correlate with the severity of the underlying condition, and hunger levels are unreliable indicators of intraabdominal disease severity.
keyPoints: Pain severity may not reflect underlying condition severity.
Hunger is an unreliable sole indicator of intraabdominal disease.
items.reference: Abdominal Pain · Danny O. Jacobs

items.subtopic: SOME MECHANISMS OF PAIN ORIGINATING IN THE ABDOMEN (part 1)
items.title: Parietal Peritoneal Inflammation and Pain Characteristics
items.content: The pain from parietal peritoneal inflammation is steady, aching, and located over the inflamed area. It's transmitted by somatic nerves and varies in intensity based on the type and amount of irritant material.
keyPoints: Pain is steady and aching, localized directly over the inflamed area.
Intensity depends on the type and amount of peritoneal exposure (e.g., sterile acidic gastric juice causes more pain than neutral feces).
Peritonitis patients typically lie quietly to avoid motion-induced pain.
items.reference: Abdominal Pain · SOME MECHANISMS OF PAIN ORIGINATING IN THE ABDOMEN (part 1)

items.subtopic: SOME MECHANISMS OF PAIN ORIGINATING IN THE ABDOMEN (part 1)
items.title: Characteristics of Pain from Hollow Viscera Obstruction
items.content: Obstruction in hollow viscera causes intermittent or colicky pain, which is less localized than parietal peritoneal irritation. Small-bowel obstruction presents as poorly localized, intermittent pain, while colonic obstruction has lesser intensity and may radiate to the lumbar region.
keyPoints: Hollow viscus obstruction elicits intermittent or colicky pain.
Small-bowel obstruction: poorly localized, periumbilical or supraumbilical pain.
Colonic obstruction: infraumbilical pain, often radiating to the lumbar region.
items.reference: Abdominal Pain · SOME MECHANISMS OF PAIN ORIGINATING IN THE ABDOMEN (part 1)

items.subtopic: SOME MECHANISMS OF PAIN ORIGINATING IN THE ABDOMEN (part 1)
items.title: Pain from Biliary and Urinary Obstruction
items.content: Biliary obstruction causes steady pain, with variations in location. Acute biliary distention leads to right upper quadrant pain. Gradual dilatation may cause mild epigastric pain. Urinary bladder obstruction results in dull suprapubic pain, while ureteral obstruction causes severe flank and radiating pain.
keyPoints: Biliary colic is steady pain; acute distention causes right upper quadrant pain.
Gradual biliary dilatation may cause mild epigastric pain.
Urinary bladder obstruction: dull, low-intensity suprapubic pain.
Ureteral obstruction: severe flank and radiating pain.
items.reference: Abdominal Pain · SOME MECHANISMS OF PAIN ORIGINATING IN THE ABDOMEN (part 1)

items.subtopic: SOME MECHANISMS OF PAIN ORIGINATING IN THE ABDOMEN (part 2) | REFERRED PAIN IN ABDOMINAL DISEASE
items.title: Recognizing Vascular Disturbances in Abdominal Pain
items.content: Vascular disturbances such as superior mesenteric artery occlusion may present with mild, continuous pain over days before severe symptoms appear. Absence of tenderness and rigidity despite significant pain is characteristic.
keyPoints: Pain from vascular disturbances can be mild and continuous for 2-3 days.
Hyperperistalsis causes early discomfort rather than peritoneal inflammation.
Characteristic: Pain out of proportion to physical findings in suspected vascular disease.
items.reference: Abdominal Pain · SOME MECHANISMS OF PAIN ORIGINATING IN THE ABDOMEN (part 2) | REFERRED PAIN IN ABDOMINAL DISEASE

items.subtopic: SOME MECHANISMS OF PAIN ORIGINATING IN THE ABDOMEN (part 2) | REFERRED PAIN IN ABDOMINAL DISEASE
items.title: Identifying Referred Pain from Thoracic Sources
items.content: Referred pain from thoracic origins may mimic abdominal emergencies. It is often accompanied by splinting of the hemithorax and respiratory lag, with pain relief upon palpation.
keyPoints: Consider intrathoracic disease in upper abdominal pain.
Referred thoracic pain diminishes during inspiration.
Palpation over referred pain area may relieve discomfort.
items.reference: Abdominal Pain · SOME MECHANISMS OF PAIN ORIGINATING IN THE ABDOMEN (part 2) | REFERRED PAIN IN ABDOMINAL DISEASE

items.subtopic: SOME MECHANISMS OF PAIN ORIGINATING IN THE ABDOMEN (part 2) | REFERRED PAIN IN ABDOMINAL DISEASE
items.title: Differentiating Referred Pain from Spinal and Genital Sources
items.content: Referred pain from the spine is intensified by motions like coughing, while genital referred pain is accentuated by pressure on the organs. Both are poorly localized.
keyPoints: Spinal referred pain involves nerve root compression or irritation.
Genital referred pain is dull and aching, worsened by organ pressure.
items.reference: Abdominal Pain · SOME MECHANISMS OF PAIN ORIGINATING IN THE ABDOMEN (part 2) | REFERRED PAIN IN ABDOMINAL DISEASE

items.title: Consider Metabolic and Immunosuppressive Etiologies in Abdominal Pain
items.content: In patients presenting with abdominal pain, a metabolic origin must be considered when the cause is obscure. This includes conditions like hyperlipidemia-associated pancreatitis or familial Mediterranean fever. In immunocompromised individuals, such as those on chemotherapy or with AIDS, atypical infections (e.g., cytomegalovirus, mycobacteria) and complications like neutropenic enterocolitis should be considered.
keyPoints: Metabolic diseases can mimic other intraabdominal conditions; e.g., hyperlipidemia may lead to pancreatitis.
Familial Mediterranean fever presents with abdominal pain as a hallmark symptom.
In immunocompromised patients, infections like cytomegalovirus and mycobacteria can cause abdominal pain.
Neutropenic enterocolitis is a potential cause of abdominal pain in chemotherapy patients.
items.reference: Abdominal Pain · METABOLIC ABDOMINAL CRISES | IMMUNOCOMPROMISE

items.title: Recognize the Impact of Immunosuppression on Abdominal Pain Diagnosis
items.content: Diagnosing abdominal pain in immunocompromised patients is challenging due to masked or absent physiologic responses. Conditions like acalculous cholecystitis and splenic abscesses should be considered, especially with pathogens such as Candida or Salmonella. Serial examinations are crucial for assessing the need for surgical intervention in these patients.
keyPoints: Immunocompromised states can mask typical physiologic responses to abdominal pathology.
Acalculous cholecystitis may occur in immunosuppressed individuals, often linked with infections like cryptosporidiosis.
Splenic abscesses due to Candida or Salmonella should be considered in left upper quadrant pain.
Serial examinations are essential for managing potential complications such as perforations.
items.reference: Abdominal Pain · METABOLIC ABDOMINAL CRISES | IMMUNOCOMPROMISE

items.title: Causalgic Pain: Recognizing Peripheral Nerve Injury
items.content: Causalgic pain, often burning in nature, indicates an injury to sensory nerves and is confined to the distribution of a specific peripheral nerve. It can be triggered by non-painful stimuli like touch or temperature changes, even at rest. The presence of irregularly spaced cutaneous 'pain spots' may suggest an old nerve injury.
keyPoints: Burning pain limited to a peripheral nerve distribution
Non-painful stimuli (touch, temperature) can cause pain
Cutaneous 'pain spots' indicate potential past nerve injury
items.reference: Abdominal Pain · NEUROGENIC CAUSES

items.title: Spinal Nerve Pain: Identifying Causes and Characteristics
items.content: Pain from spinal nerves or roots is sudden, lancinating, and not related to food intake or abdominal distention. It may result from conditions like herpes zoster, arthritis, tumors, herniated discs, diabetes, or syphilis. The pain worsens with spine movement and is confined to specific dermatomes, often accompanied by hyperesthesia.
keyPoints: Sudden, lancinating pain
Not related to food intake or abdominal distention
Causes include herpes zoster, arthritis, tumors, herniated discs, diabetes, syphilis
Worsens with spine movement, confined to specific dermatomes
Hyperesthesia is common
items.reference: Abdominal Pain · NEUROGENIC CAUSES

items.title: Urgent Management of Exsanguinating Intraabdominal Hemorrhage
items.content: In cases of exsanguinating intraabdominal hemorrhage, such as a ruptured aneurysm, immediate surgical intervention is critical. These patients should be rushed to the operating room without delay for assessment and treatment, bypassing unnecessary diagnostic procedures that could occur in radiology or emergency settings.
keyPoints: Immediate surgery required for exsanguinating intraabdominal hemorrhage
No absolute contraindications to operation when massive intraabdominal hemorrhage is present
Avoid unnecessary examinations that delay surgical intervention
items.reference: Abdominal Pain · Abdominal Pain (part 1) | Abdominal Pain (part 2)

items.title: Approach to Acute vs. Chronic Abdominal Pain
items.content: Acute abdominal pain often allows for a more straightforward diagnosis compared to chronic pain, where conditions like IBS are common. A detailed history and careful examination are crucial in both scenarios to guide appropriate management.
keyPoints: Diagnosis is more readily established with acute abdominal pain than chronic
IBS is a frequent cause of abdominal pain
Detailed patient history aids in decision-making for further diagnostic testing
items.reference: Abdominal Pain · Abdominal Pain (part 1) | Abdominal Pain (part 2)

items.title: Physical Examination Techniques and Interpretation
items.content: A gentle and thorough physical examination is essential, especially in cases of peritoneal inflammation. Techniques like gentle percussion for rebound tenderness are preferred over forceful methods to avoid misleading results due to muscle spasm.
keyPoints: Gentle and thorough examination yields more accurate assessment
Rebound tenderness can be elicited by cough or gentle percussion
Forceful palpation may induce protective spasms, obscuring true findings
items.reference: Abdominal Pain · Abdominal Pain (part 1) | Abdominal Pain (part 2)

items.question: Which statement best describes the approach to diagnosing acute abdominal pain?
options: A diagnosis of 'acute or surgical abdomen' is always acceptable.
Pain severity directly correlates with the severity of the underlying condition.
A meticulous history and physical examination are crucial for focusing the differential diagnosis.
The presence of hunger reliably indicates the severity of intraabdominal disease.
items.explanation: A meticulously executed, detailed history and physical examination are critically important for focusing the differential diagnosis.
items.reference: Abdominal Pain · Danny O. Jacobs

items.question: Which condition is NOT listed as a common cause of abdominal pain on admission?
items.explanation: The passage lists nonspecific abdominal pain, acute appendicitis, and pain of urologic origin as common causes.
items.reference: Abdominal Pain · Danny O. Jacobs

items.question: What is the significance of the term 'acute or surgical abdomen' according to the passage?
options: It accurately describes all cases requiring urgent intervention.
It is often misleading and should not be used as a diagnosis.
It indicates that pain severity correlates with disease severity.
It is synonymous with self-limited disease processes.
items.explanation: A diagnosis of 'acute or surgical abdomen' is not acceptable because of its often misleading and erroneous connotations.
items.reference: Abdominal Pain · Danny O. Jacobs

items.subtopic: SOME MECHANISMS OF PAIN ORIGINATING IN THE ABDOMEN (part 1)
items.question: Which type of peritoneal exposure causes more pain: sterile acidic gastric juice or grossly contaminated pH neutral feces?
options: Both cause the same amount of pain
items.explanation: Sudden release of sterile acidic gastric juice into the peritoneal cavity causes more pain than grossly contaminated pH neutral feces.
items.reference: Abdominal Pain · SOME MECHANISMS OF PAIN ORIGINATING IN THE ABDOMEN (part 1)

items.subtopic: SOME MECHANISMS OF PAIN ORIGINATING IN THE ABDOMEN (part 1)
items.question: What is a characteristic feature of peritoneal irritation?
options: Tonic reflex spasm of the abdominal musculature
items.explanation: Peritoneal irritation is characterized by tonic reflex spasm of the abdominal musculature.
items.reference: Abdominal Pain · SOME MECHANISMS OF PAIN ORIGINATING IN THE ABDOMEN (part 1)

items.subtopic: SOME MECHANISMS OF PAIN ORIGINATING IN THE ABDOMEN (part 1)
items.question: How does small-bowel obstruction typically present?
options: Localized, constant pain in the lower abdomen
items.explanation: Small-bowel obstruction often presents as poorly localized, intermittent periumbilical or supraumbilical pain.
items.reference: Abdominal Pain · SOME MECHANISMS OF PAIN ORIGINATING IN THE ABDOMEN (part 1)

items.subtopic: SOME MECHANISMS OF PAIN ORIGINATING IN THE ABDOMEN (part 1)
items.question: What type of pain is typically associated with sudden distention of the biliary tree?
items.explanation: Sudden distention of the biliary tree produces steady rather than colicky type of pain.
items.reference: Abdominal Pain · SOME MECHANISMS OF PAIN ORIGINATING IN THE ABDOMEN (part 1)

items.subtopic: SOME MECHANISMS OF PAIN ORIGINATING IN THE ABDOMEN (part 2) | REFERRED PAIN IN ABDOMINAL DISEASE
items.question: Which of the following is characteristic of occlusion of the superior mesenteric artery?
options: Severe pain with immediate peritoneal inflammation.
Mild continuous or cramping diffuse pain for several days before vascular collapse.
Constant and aching abdominal wall pain accentuated by movement.
Referred pain to the sacral region due to rectus sheath hematoma.
items.explanation: Occlusion of the superior mesenteric artery often presents with mild continuous or cramping diffuse pain for days before more severe symptoms appear.
items.reference: Abdominal Pain · SOME MECHANISMS OF PAIN ORIGINATING IN THE ABDOMEN (part 2) | REFERRED PAIN IN ABDOMINAL DISEASE

items.subtopic: SOME MECHANISMS OF PAIN ORIGINATING IN THE ABDOMEN (part 2) | REFERRED PAIN IN ABDOMINAL DISEASE
items.question: What should the presence of abdominal pain with radiation to the sacral region, flank, or genitalia suggest?
options: Compression or irritation of nerve roots.
items.explanation: Radiation of pain to these areas can indicate a rupturing abdominal aortic aneurysm.
items.reference: Abdominal Pain · SOME MECHANISMS OF PAIN ORIGINATING IN THE ABDOMEN (part 2) | REFERRED PAIN IN ABDOMINAL DISEASE

items.subtopic: SOME MECHANISMS OF PAIN ORIGINATING IN THE ABDOMEN (part 2) | REFERRED PAIN IN ABDOMINAL DISEASE
items.question: Which of the following is NOT a feature of referred pain from thoracic origin?
options: Splinting of the involved hemithorax with respiratory lag.
Pain accentuated by palpation over the area in the abdomen.
Decrease in excursion more marked than intraabdominal disease.
Apparent abdominal muscle spasm diminishes during inspiration.
items.explanation: Referred pain from thoracic origin does not usually accentuate with palpation over the area of referred pain in the abdomen.
items.reference: Abdominal Pain · SOME MECHANISMS OF PAIN ORIGINATING IN THE ABDOMEN (part 2) | REFERRED PAIN IN ABDOMINAL DISEASE

items.subtopic: SOME MECHANISMS OF PAIN ORIGINATING IN THE ABDOMEN (part 2) | REFERRED PAIN IN ABDOMINAL DISEASE
items.question: What is a common characteristic of abdominal wall pain?
options: Constant and aching discomfort accentuated by movement.
Referred to the sacral region due to vascular disturbances.
Severe pain with immediate peritoneal inflammation.
items.explanation: Abdominal wall pain is usually constant, aching, and worsened by movement or pressure.
items.reference: Abdominal Pain · SOME MECHANISMS OF PAIN ORIGINATING IN THE ABDOMEN (part 2) | REFERRED PAIN IN ABDOMINAL DISEASE

items.question: Which of the following conditions is associated with severe abdominal pain and can be mistaken for intestinal obstruction due to hyperpertalsis?
items.explanation: The pain of porphyria and lead colic is difficult to distinguish from intestinal obstruction due to severe hyperpertalsis.
items.reference: Abdominal Pain · METABOLIC ABDOMINAL CRISES | IMMUNOCOMPROMISE

items.question: In immunocompromised patients, which of the following pathogens can cause abdominal pain by affecting all gastrointestinal organs?
items.explanation: Unusual infections in immunocompromised patients may include cytomegalovirus, affecting all gastrointestinal organs.
items.reference: Abdominal Pain · METABOLIC ABDOMINAL CRISES | IMMUNOCOMPROMISE

items.question: Which condition should be considered when evaluating a patient with left upper quadrant pain and a history of immunosuppression?
options: Splenic abscess due to Candida or Salmonella infection
items.explanation: In immunocompromised patients, splenic abscesses due to Candida or Salmonella should be considered with left upper quadrant pain.
items.reference: Abdominal Pain · METABOLIC ABDOMINAL CRISES | IMMUNOCOMPROMISE

items.question: Which of the following is characteristic of causalgic pain due to sensory nerve injury?
options: Pain has a burning character and may be present at rest.
Pain is associated with food intake.
items.explanation: Causalgic pain due to sensory nerve injury is characterized by a burning sensation that can occur even at rest and may be triggered by non-painful stimuli like touch or temperature changes.
items.reference: Abdominal Pain · NEUROGENIC CAUSES

items.question: What type of pain is associated with spinal nerve or root injuries?
options: Lancinating pain that comes and goes suddenly.
items.explanation: Spinal nerve or root injuries cause lancinating pain that is sudden and intermittent, often associated with conditions like herpes zoster or herniated discs.
items.reference: Abdominal Pain · NEUROGENIC CAUSES

items.question: Which statement best describes the nature of abdominal pain in irritable bowel syndrome (IBS)?
options: Pain is consistently related to food intake.
Pain is localized and accompanied by severe muscle spasms.
Pain varies considerably in type and location, often triggered by stress.
Pain is always associated with nausea and vomiting.
items.explanation: In IBS, abdominal pain varies in type and location and can be brought on by stress; it does not consistently relate to food intake or cause severe muscle spasms.
items.reference: Abdominal Pain · NEUROGENIC CAUSES

items.question: Which condition requires immediate operative intervention without an orderly approach?
items.explanation: Exsanguinating intraabdominal hemorrhage, such as a ruptured aneurysm, requires immediate surgery without delay.
items.reference: Abdominal Pain · Abdominal Pain (part 1) | Abdominal Pain (part 2)

items.question: What is the most common cause of abdominal pain that must always be considered?
items.explanation: Irritable Bowel Syndrome (IBS) is one of the most common causes of abdominal pain.
items.reference: Abdominal Pain · Abdominal Pain (part 1) | Abdominal Pain (part 2)

items.question: What should be done with narcotics or analgesics in patients with acute abdominal pain?
options: Administer only after a definitive diagnosis
Withhold until further testing is complete
Do not withhold until a definitive plan has been formulated
Avoid entirely to prevent obfuscation of the diagnosis
items.explanation: Narcotics or analgesics should not be withheld until a definitive diagnosis or plan is made.
items.reference: Abdominal Pain · Abdominal Pain (part 1) | Abdominal Pain (part 2)

items.question: What can be misleading when assessing peritoneal inflammation?
items.explanation: A brusque examination can make it difficult for subsequent examiners to accurately assess peritoneal inflammation.
items.reference: Abdominal Pain · Abdominal Pain (part 1) | Abdominal Pain (part 2)

items.statement: Pain severity always correlates with the severity of the underlying condition.
items.explanation: The passage states that pain severity does not necessarily correlate with the severity of the underlying condition.
items.reference: Abdominal Pain · Danny O. Jacobs

items.statement: A detailed history and physical examination are important for evaluating patients with acute abdominal pain.
items.explanation: The passage emphasizes that a meticulously executed, detailed history and physical examination are critically important.
items.reference: Abdominal Pain · Danny O. Jacobs

items.subtopic: SOME MECHANISMS OF PAIN ORIGINATING IN THE ABDOMEN (part 1)
items.statement: The absence of cramping discomfort in hollow viscus distention can be misleading.
items.explanation: Distention of a hollow viscus may produce steady pain with only rare paroxysms, making the absence of cramping discomfort misleading.
items.reference: Abdominal Pain · SOME MECHANISMS OF PAIN ORIGINATING IN THE ABDOMEN (part 1)

items.subtopic: SOME MECHANISMS OF PAIN ORIGINATING IN THE ABDOMEN (part 1)
items.statement: Peritonitis is characterized by thrashing in discomfort.
items.explanation: Patients with peritonitis characteristically lie quietly to avoid motion, unlike those with colic who may thrash.
items.reference: Abdominal Pain · SOME MECHANISMS OF PAIN ORIGINATING IN THE ABDOMEN (part 1)

items.subtopic: SOME MECHANISMS OF PAIN ORIGINATING IN THE ABDOMEN (part 2) | REFERRED PAIN IN ABDOMINAL DISEASE
items.statement: Referred pain from the spine is usually associated with hyperesthesia over involved dermatomes.
items.explanation: Spinal referred pain often involves compression or irritation of nerve roots, leading to hyperesthesia.
items.reference: Abdominal Pain · SOME MECHANISMS OF PAIN ORIGINATING IN THE ABDOMEN (part 2) | REFERRED PAIN IN ABDOMINAL DISEASE

items.subtopic: SOME MECHANISMS OF PAIN ORIGINATING IN THE ABDOMEN (part 2) | REFERRED PAIN IN ABDOMINAL DISEASE
items.statement: Pain referred from the thorax is usually accompanied by increased abdominal muscle spasm during inspiration.
items.explanation: Referred pain from the thorax often results in decreased abdominal muscle spasm during inspiration.
items.reference: Abdominal Pain · SOME MECHANISMS OF PAIN ORIGINATING IN THE ABDOMEN (part 2) | REFERRED PAIN IN ABDOMINAL DISEASE

items.statement: Diabetic acidosis can be precipitated by acute appendicitis or intestinal obstruction.
items.explanation: Diabetic acidosis may be triggered by conditions like acute appendicitis or intestinal obstruction.
items.reference: Abdominal Pain · METABOLIC ABDOMINAL CRISES | IMMUNOCOMPROMISE

items.statement: Normal physiologic responses are always present in immunocompromised patients.
items.explanation: In immunocompromised patients, normal physiologic responses may be absent or masked.
items.reference: Abdominal Pain · METABOLIC ABDOMINAL CRISES | IMMUNOCOMPROMISE

items.statement: Causalgic pain due to sensory nerve injury is typically associated with abdominal distention.
items.explanation: Causalgic pain from sensory nerve injury is not associated with abdominal distention.
items.reference: Abdominal Pain · NEUROGENIC CAUSES

items.statement: Pain due to spinal nerve injuries can be relieved by movement of the spine.
items.explanation: Pain from spinal nerve injuries is usually made worse, not relieved, by movement of the spine.
items.reference: Abdominal Pain · NEUROGENIC CAUSES

items.statement: Peristaltic sounds are the most revealing aspect of a physical examination for abdominal pain.
items.explanation: Auscultation of the abdomen is one of the least revealing aspects in evaluating abdominal pain.
items.reference: Abdominal Pain · Abdominal Pain (part 1) | Abdominal Pain (part 2)

items.statement: Laboratory examinations often establish a diagnosis for patients with abdominal pain.
items.explanation: Laboratory exams rarely establish a diagnosis; they are valuable but not definitive.
items.reference: Abdominal Pain · Abdominal Pain (part 1) | Abdominal Pain (part 2)

items.subtopic: SOME MECHANISMS OF PAIN ORIGINATING IN THE ABDOMEN (part 1)
items.assertion: Peritoneal pain is accentuated by pressure or movement.
items.reason: The peritoneum's response to palpation or movement increases the intensity of pain.
items.explanation: Both assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Abdominal Pain · SOME MECHANISMS OF PAIN ORIGINATING IN THE ABDOMEN (part 1)

items.subtopic: SOME MECHANISMS OF PAIN ORIGINATING IN THE ABDOMEN (part 1)
items.assertion: Gradual dilatation of the biliary tree often causes severe pain.
items.reason: Carcinoma of the head of the pancreas can lead to gradual dilatation without significant pain.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because gradual dilatation may cause no or only mild pain, while the reason is true.
items.reference: Abdominal Pain · SOME MECHANISMS OF PAIN ORIGINATING IN THE ABDOMEN (part 1)

items.subtopic: SOME MECHANISMS OF PAIN ORIGINATING IN THE ABDOMEN (part 2) | REFERRED PAIN IN ABDOMINAL DISEASE
items.assertion: Pain from the testes or seminal vesicles is generally accentuated by pressure.
items.reason: The abdominal discomfort experienced is of dull, aching character and poorly localized.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true; the nature of pain from these organs aligns with their description.
items.reference: Abdominal Pain · SOME MECHANISMS OF PAIN ORIGINATING IN THE ABDOMEN (part 2) | REFERRED PAIN IN ABDOMINAL DISEASE

items.subtopic: SOME MECHANISMS OF PAIN ORIGINATING IN THE ABDOMEN (part 2) | REFERRED PAIN IN ABDOMINAL DISEASE
items.assertion: Referred pain from the thorax is often accompanied by increased abdominal muscle spasm during inspiration.
items.reason: This is because intrathoracic diseases mimic intraabdominal emergencies.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; referred thoracic pain usually results in decreased abdominal muscle spasm during inspiration.
items.reference: Abdominal Pain · SOME MECHANISMS OF PAIN ORIGINATING IN THE ABDOMEN (part 2) | REFERRED PAIN IN ABDOMINAL DISEASE

items.assertion: Neutropenic enterocolitis is a cause of abdominal pain in patients with bone marrow suppression.
items.reason: It is often identified as a cause of abdominal pain and fever due to chemotherapy-induced bone marrow suppression.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Abdominal Pain · METABOLIC ABDOMINAL CRISES | IMMUNOCOMPROMISE

items.assertion: Irritable bowel syndrome (IBS) pain can be triggered by stress.
items.reason: The causes of IBS are not fully understood, but proinflammatory cells and lipotoxic lipids likely play a role.
items.explanation: While the assertion is true that IBS pain can be triggered by stress, the reason given does not directly explain why stress triggers the pain.
items.reference: Abdominal Pain · NEUROGENIC CAUSES

items.assertion: A normal white blood cell count can be observed in cases of intestinal infarction.
items.reason: Leukocytosis is not always present with abdominal conditions like intestinal infarction.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why a normal white blood cell count can be seen in intestinal infarction.
items.reference: Abdominal Pain · Abdominal Pain (part 1) | Abdominal Pain (part 2)

items.assertion: Rebound tenderness elicited by sudden release of a deeply palpating hand is necessary for diagnosing peritonitis.
items.reason: Gentle percussion can provide the same information as rebound tenderness without causing patient discomfort.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because gentle percussion, not sudden release, is recommended; however, the reason is true.
items.reference: Abdominal Pain · Abdominal Pain (part 1) | Abdominal Pain (part 2)
